summary: DHH argues that Silicon Valley's obsession with 'changing the world' is a delusional coping mechanism that most companies use to justify grueling work cultures. He points out that companies which actually changed the world (Twitter, Facebook) started with mundane goals, not grand missions. Using HealthIQ's career page as a case study, he mocks a life insurance company claiming to 'heal the world' and argues this cognitive dissonance is an existential cry for help. He concludes that embracing the mundane nature of most work is healthier than carrying the impossible burden of world-changing ambitions.
key_points: Companies that actually changed the world (Twitter, Facebook) started with modest, unglamorous goals — not missions to reshape civilization
The 'changing the world' rhetoric is a coping mechanism to justify brutal work conditions that wouldn't be warranted by the company's actual product
Steve Jobs's 'sugar water' pitch to Scully has been distorted into a toxic belief that all work must have cosmic significance to have meaning
Most people's legacy will be the impressions they leave on friends, family, and colleagues — and that's cause for celebration, not despair
The cognitive dissonance between mundane products and grandiose missions eventually cracks, and the harder you invest in the delusion, the harder you shatter
money_quotes: If everyone in Silicon Valley who proclaimed to be on a mission to change the world actually did, our collective regret would be far deeper.
What on earth would prompt someone hawking life insurance — LIFE INSURANCE! — to posture, and quite possibly believe, that they're Changing The World™?

If you're going to toil your life away on a literal treadmill with meetings at 9pm every night, you're going to need to tell yourself something different than 'I do this such that I can save privileged joggers and kale eaters 10% on life insurance'.
The idea that work isn't worth living unless it entails the prospect of upsetting the world order.

Carrying the weight of Changing The World on your shoulders is a tremendous burden. Look at the characters who actually managed it. Most of them weren't exactly living envious lives outside the scope of their work.
You can't outrun existential angst. Even if you put actual fucking treadmills in the middle of your office.
Set out to do good work. Set out to be fair in your dealings with customers, employees, and reality.
Chances are you won't, and if you do, it's not going to be because you said you would.
key_insight: The grandiose 'changing the world' rhetoric in Silicon Valley is a coping mechanism that masks mundane reality, and embracing the ordinary nature of most work leads to a healthier, more honest existence.
